text: 6 Minute English from BBCLearningEnglish.com Hello, this is 6 Minute English from BBCLearningEnglish.
I'm Neil. And I'm Georgie.
If you've heard about meat-free Mondays or veganery, you probably know about a trend which an increasing number of people are trying to reduce or even stop eating meat.
This is often for health, animal welfare, or environmental reasons.
Instead, people are getting their daily amount of protein through plant-based alternatives.
Meat-free sausages and burgers made using ingredients like soy, tofu, or fungi, as well as temper, beans or nuts.
Have you tried any of these plant-based meat alternatives Georgie?
Yes, I have.
I have lots of vegetarian friends, so I've been to veggie barbecues and have tried the plant-based sausages and burgers.
I quite like them, as long as they're covered in sauce.
What about you?
Yeah, I think I agree.
They're fine. There's a reason for them, but I'm not sure they're as good as the real thing.

Now, with processed meat being linked to diseases like diabetes and cancer, most doctors agree that plant-based options are healthier, but some people are worried about how plant-based alternatives are made, including radio listener Graham Glover, who asked this question to BBC Radio 4 program sliced bread.
Well, my question is, looking at these vegetable sausages, how they are processed, full of additives and flare rings and callarings, are they any healthier than a pork sausage?
In this episode, we'll be finding out whether plant-based foods really are a healthier alternative to meat, and as usual, we'll be learning some useful new words and phrases.
Remember, you can find all the vocabulary along with a quiz and a worksheet on our website, BBCLearningEnglish.com.
But now I have a question for you, Georgie, listener Graham loves eating sausages.
But do you know which of the following words means sausages in British English slang?
Is it A, booze, B, banger or C, brunch?
So I actually know the answer to this question, so I'm not going to say.
Otherwise, it will spoil it for our listeners.
Okay, well, we'll all find out at the end of the program.
Although most research agrees that plant-based foods are healthier than meat, it's also true that meat-free products are processed.
To make meat-free sausages, for example, after the proteins are broken down, flavorings and callarings are added to reproduce the taste or texture of meat.
Corn, one of the best known brands of meat-free products, uses microprotein, a fungi-based protein similar to the fungi used in the fermentation of yogurt and bread.
So, what does listener Graham think?
Let's find out as he talks to Greg Furt, presenter of BBC Radio 4's Sliced Bread.

Graham, listening to all this, normally a fan of a meat sausage, your plant-based meat alternative sausage curious that we're exploring today, what do you think about hearing about the sources of where they come from?
What's your gut reaction to hearing that corn comes from a fungus?
I have no problems with that.
It's just another form of protein, as far as I'm concerned.
It's what's the healthiest.
Yes, we will get to health.
I think that's the big conversation for today.
Greg asked Graham for his gut reaction.
Your gut reaction is your opinion based on your initial feeling or instinct about something.
It can't be explained logically.
Actually, Graham doesn't mind that his sausage protein is a fungus, as long as it's healthy.
He uses the phrase, as far as I'm concerned, to indicate that he is giving his own opinion on the subject.
Of course, healthy eating is important, but there are other factors influencing whether meat lovers like Graham could be persuaded to try plant-based alternatives.
These include things like the taste, color, and texture of the sausage.
In other words, whether a plant-based sausage looks and feels like real meat.
Here's Greg discussing this point for BBC Radio 4 program sliced bread.
It's interesting, isn't it, because my mum is a long-time veggie, and we were talking about this, and she says, I don't want my alternative proteins to resemble meat.
It's one of the reasons she says she doesn't eat meat.
But you, Graham, you said you do want your plant-based sausage to resemble a pork sausage?
Yeah, I'm obviously in the meat eater, but I do need a plant-based product to be similar in terms of flavor, texture, and appearance.
Graham wants his plant-based sausages to resemble, meaning looks similar to meat, but that's not the case for Greg's mum, who's a long-time veggie.
A veggie is slang for a vegetarian, a person who doesn't eat meat, and the adjective long-time describes someone who has been or done a particular thing for many years.
So a long-time veggie is someone who's been vegetarian for many years.
Whether you want your veggie sausages to look, taste, and feel like meat, or you prefer your protein from a plant, now there's every chance you'll find what you're looking for.
And keep your body and planet healthy as well.
Now, isn't it time you revealed the answer to your question, Neil?
Yes, I asked what the British English slang word for sausages is, and what is it, Georgie?
The answer is banger, and we have a typical dish in the UK called bangers and mash, which is sausages and mash.
Love it, with gravy.
Okay, let's recap the vocabulary we've learned starting with banger, a word which is slang for sausage.
Your gut reaction is your opinion about something based on instinctive feelings, not logic or reason.
The phrase as far as I'm concerned can be used to give your opinion about something.
To resemble means to look or be similar to something.
A veggie is slang for a vegetarian, someone who doesn't eat meat.
And finally, the adjective long-time can be used to say that someone has done a particular thing for many years.
Once again, our six minutes are up, but remember you can find worksheets, quizzes, and loads more resources to improve your English over on our website, bbclearningenglish.com.
See you there soon, but for now, goodbye.
Goodbye.
